# Toward Transparency: The Need for Standardized ESG Metrics

As environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors have become increasingly important to investors, the demand for high-quality, standardized ESG data has grown exponentially. Hedge funds, asset managers, and other buy-side firms are under mounting pressure from clients, regulators, and the public to integrate ESG data into both the investment and reporting processes. Unfortunately, current ESG data and metrics remain highly fragmented, inconsistent, and often unreliable, which presents a significant challenge for buy-side firms looking to manage ESG risks and opportunities effectively.

**The Challenges**

One of the primary issues buy-side firms face is the lack of a universal, standardized framework for ESG data and metrics. While there are numerous ESG rating agencies, data providers, and sustainability reporting standards, each uses its own methodologies and approaches. This results in widely divergent assessments of any one company’s ESG performance. This lack of consistency makes it extremely difficult for investors to compare metrics and make informed decisions.

Within this confusing mix of metrics and scores, there is also a lack of clarity about how the numbers are calculated. This lack of standardization not only complicates the due diligence and investment processes but also undermines funds’ ability to effectively report on the ESG performance of portfolios.

Another challenge is the quality and reliability of ESG data itself. Much of the information used to assess a company’s ESG practices is self-reported, often with limited third-party verification or assurance. This can lead to greenwashing, when companies present an exaggerated or otherwise misleading picture of their sustainability efforts. ESG data also tends to be backward-looking, or focused on historical performance, rather than forward-looking, or indicative of a company’s future ESG risks and opportunities.

Resources devoted to ESG data is another major pain point for the buy side. Funds and asset managers spend an enormous amount of time and energy trying to validate ESG data, reconcile disparate sources, and fill in the gaps. This diverts attention away from the more strategic aspects of ESG integration and portfolio construction.

**The Importance of Standardized ESG Metrics**

Addressing these challenges is critical for funds and asset managers incorporating ESG factors into investment decision-making and reporting. Standardized, reliable ESG metrics would provide a common language and framework investors can use to assess and compare the sustainability of companies and investment products.

This is why standardized ESG metrics could be a game changer for the asset management industry. Standardized ESG metrics would allow funds to benchmark companies and portfolios against their peers, identify material risks and opportunities more accurately, and make more informed, data-driven investment decisions. This would enhance the ability to deliver on fiduciary duty to clients and meet the growing demand for sustainable investing solutions.

Beyond the benefits for individual firms, standardized ESG metrics would promote greater transparency and accountability in the broader financial system. Consistent, reliable ESG data would empower investors to hold companies and asset managers more accountable for sustainability claims and performance, driving real progress on critical environmental and social issues.

**The Path to Standardization**

Recognizing the pressing need for ESG data standardization, various organizations and initiatives have emerged to address the challenge. One prominent example is the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), established by the IFRS Foundation in 2021. The ISSB is working to develop a comprehensive global baseline of sustainability-related disclosure standards to provide investors with high-quality information about companies’ sustainability-related risks and opportunities.

Another initiative is the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB), which has developed industry-specific sustainability accounting standards to guide companies in their ESG reporting. SASB’s standards have been widely adopted by large public companies and are increasingly being incorporated into the investment processes of buy-side firms.

At the regional level, the European Union has taken a leading role in driving ESG data standardization through the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR) and the development of the EU Taxonomy, a classification system for sustainable economic activities. These regulations aim to provide investors with more reliable, comparable information on the sustainability characteristics of financial products and the underlying companies.

**Standardization: A Work in Progress**

While these and other efforts are encouraging, the journey toward truly standardized, globally harmonized ESG metrics is still a work in progress. Buy-side firms must continue to advocate for and actively participate in the development of these standards, ensuring that the unique needs and perspectives of the buy side are represented.

Funds and asset managers have a crucial role to play in shaping the future of ESG data and metrics. Firms should take every opportunity to engage with standard-setting bodies, data providers, and regulators to ensure that forthcoming ESG data standards are fit for purpose and address the real-world challenges funds face with respect to integrating ESG factors into investment processes.

In the meantime, buy-side firms should explore technology-based solutions to help manage the complexities of ESG data. ESG data management platforms can assist in aggregating, normalizing, and analyzing ESG data from multiple sources, while advanced analytics and artificial intelligence can help identify material ESG risks and opportunities more effectively. By taking a proactive, collaborative approach to ESG data standardization and leveraging the latest ESG data management solutions, buy-side firms can navigate the evolving ESG landscape and deliver superior, sustainable investment outcomes for clients.

**How IVP Can Help**

The [IVP ESG Management](https://www.ivp.in/products/esg-management/) platform is a comprehensive solution specifically designed for buy-side firms. It provides all the capabilities funds need to source, manage, and effectively utilize ESG data for investing and reporting. By leveraging the IVP platform for ESG data management, firms can streamline portfolio construction, enhance asset allocation, strengthen risk management and compliance practices, and ensure accurate regulatory reporting. In addition, IVP offers an ESG Center of Excellence that serves as a valuable resource for funds seeking to establish a reliable framework for ESG investing.

Learn more about the [IVP ESG Management](https://www.ivp.in/products/esg-management/) platform or contact [sales@ivp.in](mailto:sales@ivp.in) to schedule a live or online demo.

--- SOURCE 2: [PDF] ESG Metric Variance and Methodology Standardization ---
URL: https://scholars.unh.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1751&context=honors

SUMMARY:
Standardization of ESG scoring methodologies will allow for a more clear-cut view of what ESG covers and how companies should be rated accordingly. Previous

FULL CONTENT:
University of New Hampshire University of New Hampshire University of New Hampshire Scholars' Repository University of New Hampshire Scholars' Repository Honors Theses and Capstones Student Scholarship Spring 2023 ESG Metric Variance and Methodology Standardization ESG Metric Variance and Methodology Standardization Jordan Moyer University of New Hampshire, Durham Follow this and additional works at: https://scholars.unh.edu/honors Part of the Business Analytics Commons Recommended Citation Recommended Citation Moyer, Jordan, "ESG Metric Variance and Methodology Standardization" (2023). Honors Theses and Capstones. 754. https://scholars.unh.edu/honors/754 This Senior Honors Thesis is brought to you for free and open access by the Student Scholarship at University of New Hampshire Scholars' Repository. It has been accepted for inclusion in Honors Theses and Capstones by an authorized administrator of University of New Hampshire Scholars' Repository. For more information, please contact Scholarly.Communication@unh.edu. Moyer1 ESG Metric Variance and Methodology Standardization Prepared by: Jordan Moyer University of New Hampshire: Peter T. Paul College of Business & Economics Advised by: Jing Wang Moyer2 Table of Contents Abstract ......................................................................................................................................................... 2 Introduction .................................................................................................................................................. 3 Literature Review .......................................................................................................................................... 5 Current CSR Reporting Practices ................................................................................................................... 8 MSCI Methodology ....................................................................................................................................... 9 Sustainalytics Methodology ........................................................................................................................... 11 Research Questions & Predictions .............................................................................................................. 11 Methodology ............................................................................................................................................... 12 Data Collection: ....................................................................................................................................... 12 Approach ................................................................................................................................................. 13 Advantages .............................................................................................................................................. 16 Limitations .............................................................................................................................................. 17 Analysis ....................................................................................................................................................... 18 Findings & Recommendations .................................................................................................................... 22 Appendix ..................................................................................................................................................... 26 Abstract Across the globe Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) scoring has become a tool used for mission-aligned investment. In the United States, there is no standardized method for scoring companies based on ESG practices; it is all completed by private companies with their own ways of managing data and creating scores. Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) reporting, though mandatory in some countries, is also completely voluntary in the United States. CSR reports are a key part of the data collection used for ESG scoring. This research looks to understand how much CSR reporting is being influenced by ESG scoring methodologies, as well as what categories are lacking in reports from companies but are included in scoring. The main findings from this analysis show the need for standardized CSR reporting as well as a Moyer3 standardized, transparent rating system to be of most use to end users like investors looking to inform mission-aligned investments. Introduction The investment industry has seen a rise in demand for socially responsible investment strategies. In response to this, ESG scoring metrics have been developed to aid in the analysis of companies' ESG efforts. Many ESG scoring systems have been developed in recent years, with some of the most notable being Sustainalytics, MSCI, CDP, and Bloomberg. Such scoring systems have the advantage of improving transparency about companies' ESG practices and increasing accountability to encourage companies to improve their ESG performance (Global Sustainable Investment Review, 2018). These advantages in turn will hopefully lead to better risk management to help investors identify and manage ESG-related risks as well as positive changes in areas such as climate change, social justice, and corporate governance, benefiting society and the environment (Giese, Lee, Melas, Nagy, & Nishikawa, 2019; Clark, Feiner, & Viehs, 2015). Despite their advantages, ESG scoring systems may have unintended consequences, such as "greenwashing" and issues with the lack of standardization and comparability between metrics. “Greenwashing” has been documented by prior research as a means for impression and image framing by companies and can take various forms, such as exaggerating the environmental benefits of a product or service, making vague or unsupported claims about a company's commitment to sustainability, or highlighting positive aspects of its ESG performance while ignoring other issues. Depending on the score provider chosen, the same company may be ranked differently, indicating the lack of standardization and comparability between ESG scoring systems. A common example of this is the comparison of ESG scores given to Tesla in 2018 and Moyer4 2019. This study found that “Tesla held an exemplary “AA” rating from MSCI, whereas FTSE ranked the company very low and Sustainalytics put the firm in the middle” (Dimson, Marsh, and Staunton 2020). Given that these scores are used to aid investors’ investment strategy decisions, the inconsistent ESG ranking of the same company across the scoring systems may lead to suboptimal investment decisions. Given that companies' corporate social responsibility (CSR) reports are typically used in the ESG scoring process, this research aims to investigate the possibility of CSR reports potentially being used as a tool for “greenwashing” and how CSR reporting can be improved to mitigate the challenge of “greenwashing”. Specifically, it examines the degree to which companies’ CSR reports are influenced by the top ESG scoring systems by comparing the topics covered in companies’ CSR reports to the dimensions/factors used in the major ESG scoring systems. This investigation helps to reveal the degree to which CSR report topics are aligned with the dimensions/factors used in the major ESG scoring systems, as well as the possibility of certain dimensions/factors being highlighted in the report while others being ignored or downplayed. A close alignment would indicate a strong influence from the ESG scoring system on the CSR report, and a high risk that CSR reports can be used by companies to frame the issue in order to score high in the ESG scoring systems. A close alignment coupled with inconsistent coverage of the different dimensions/factors may be an indicator that CSR reports may have already been used as a “greenwashing” tool for companies to highlight positive aspects but downplay the negative aspects. The research future proposes a more all-encompassing standardized ESG scoring system and CSR reporting requirements as a potential mitigation to counter the risk of CSR reporting being used as a greenwashing tool. Moyer5 As ESG rating systems are a relatively new development within the last twenty years, there has been limited research done to effectively communicate the intricate workings of each methodology and how it compares across different rating systems (Atkins, 2022). It is important that research be conducted on the comparability of ratings and their methodologies because greenwashing is becoming increasingly prevalent, and without regulations and standards set for reporting, there is no end in sight. In the common case where these metrics are being used to inform investment decisions and ensure client needs are met, there needs to be a clear definition of what makes a company a good ESG investment. Standardization of ESG scoring methodologies will allow for a more clear-cut view of what ESG covers and how companies should be rated accordingly. Previous studies largely focus on the comparability of overall company scores from top rating agencies. The data exploration and analysis performed in this report have not been evaluated in any other study to date. In light of the findings of previous studies that rating systems are incomparable, the following research further explores these rating systems against the CSR reports of those companies often rated. The relationship between company reports and rating methodologies that is being assessed in this report can lead to a better understanding of the rating systems being used, company reporting, and how greenwashing can be a major challenge without standardization and regulation of reporting and scoring. Literature Review ESG ratings have become an increasingly controversial topic, especially in the investment industry. Across the ESG rating industry, there are a multitude of score providers that are each being used, such as MSCI, Sustainalytics, Bloomberg, and ASSET4. However, the integrity and comparability of these scores have become increasingly debated. The article Moyer6 Measuring the level and risk of corporate responsibility – An empirical comparison of different ESG rating approaches began to assess the question of comparability between rating systems. This article looked to initially compare the scores of companies based on ASSET4, Bloomberg, and KLD (MSCI) ESG ratings. Overall, the findings of this research were that none of these ESG scores, which are widely used across the investment industry, are comparable with one another. This means that often companies with high ESG scores from one rating have low ESG scores from another, which can be extremely misleading unless you are well educated on exactly the methodology each is using to create the scores, which many users are not (Dorfleitner, 2015). Although this study established a foundational understanding that aggregate scores are not directly comparable, it does have some limitations due to its limited comparison of three providers and the lack of information provided about the methodologies used. Additional research about the reasoning behind the incomparability of scores across providers is another limitation that should be researched more fully. Another aspect that has been increasingly controversial in ESG ratings and reporting is the difference between scores when broken down into E, S, and G categories. In Divergent ESG Ratings, the authors analyzed Sustainalytics, MSCI, and FTSE’s metrics and their ratings of different companies. This further emphasized the issue at hand, where none of the ratings were similar and most methodologies were very different when analyzed further into the topics under the environmental, social, and governance categories. At a broad level, ESG scores had some minor similarities, but when broken down into E, S, and G, there were little to no similarities (Dimson, 2020). This is extremely important to note because, taken out of context, these broad ESG scores can be very misleading without knowing exactly what is being factored into scoring. Moyer7 Each pillar (E, S, and G) are scored based on different criteria, especially depending on the provider that is doing the scoring. The article Scoring Environment Pillar In Environmental, Social, And Governance (ESG) Assessment evaluated mainly the MSCI and Sustainalytics methodologies for the Environment Pillar. This study created a list of commonly used topics for each environment score and again found limited comparability between methodologies (Senadheera, 2021). This has led to even more concerns about the use of ESG scores in investment decisions. If the users of these scores are not entirely in line with and aware of the methodology of their provider, they could be easily misled and be better served by a different methodology. The transparency of scoring methodologies is lacking, making it very difficult to really know why the scores are what they are for many average users, leading us again to the problem of inconsistency in scores. Another gray area that allows for deceptive scores are the data sources that each rating system uses. Currently, each score provider draws data from a variety of sources, and each provider also handles missing data differently. The general practices of each rating system show that data can be collected from publicly available materials, CSR reports, and individual interviews. This mix of collection types has led to providers scoring using different and possibly incomplete data. In addition to this, missing data is handled differently with each rating system. Some systems provide industry average scores for missing information and others give zeros, both of which impact scoring and how a company is perceived. While this study did not discuss in depth the methodologies used, it further emphasizes the lack of standardization across scores and creates a new question about where providers should be getting their information to base scores (Dimson., 2020). Moyer8 The article The Influence of Firm Size on the ESG Score: Corporate Sustainability Ratings Under Review placed additional emphasis on the significance of data availability in relation to ESG scoring. This source analyzed the impact of the size of the firm compared to ESG scoring as well as the effects of data availability. Based on the overall findings, the article confirms “the highly significant relationship between data availability and ESG scores in all sectors” (Drempetic, 2020). The need for standardization of data collection and reporting methods is also a challenge that has significant implications for ESG investing. According to the S&P Global article Lack of standardized ESG data may hide material risks, OECD says the article finds that this issue could significantly “hinder the growth of ESG investing” (Laidlaw, 2020). Without future regulations and standardized methods of reporting ESG data, ESG scoring will continue to be vulnerable to the possibility of misrepresenting companies due to unavailable or incorrect data. Overall, the literature that has previously been completed emphasizes the argument that data availability is crucial, and that current scoring systems are not comparable between one another, both of which are key challenges that need to be addressed as ESG scoring and reporting evolve. Current CSR Reporting Practices Currently, in the United States, CSR reports are entirely voluntary for companies to provide. In other countries such as the UK, Australia and Canada, there are regulations and requirements for large organizations to report on the environmental and social impacts of their business (Envizi, 2023). There is a possibility that in the future reporting will become regulated within the US, however, there are no concrete plans for this to occur yet. Due to the lack of regulations in the US, private companies have taken the opportunity to make their own standards for reporting, which can be used by companies to aid in their ESG Moyer9 reporting practices. Some examples of the most widely used standards come from the Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), the Dow Jones Sustainability Index and the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) (Envizi, 2023). Companies have the option to become signatories to these companies and follow their reporting standards as an example of their dedication to ESG practices, but this is strictly voluntary at this time. MSCI Methodology Morgan Stanley Capital International, also referred to as MSCI, ESG ratings are one of the most widely used methodologies in the US and across the globe. The reason for this is due to its broad range of categories assessed, weighted scores within each industry, weekly score updates, annual company reviews, and many additional benefits. This methodology consists of the following topics: Moyer10 Figure 1: MSCI Categories (MSCI ESG Research LLC. 2023) As seen above, the MSCI methodology is broad in nature, covering over thirty topics in relation to environmental, social and governance practices. This methodology aims to mitigate the risk of categories having different materiality within each industry by creating weighted scores to better reflect companies’ performance within each industry. In addition, it manages data collection by using publicly available resources as well as allowing for companies to verify data and having meetings with each company yearly. (MSCI ESG Research LLC., 2023.) Sustainalytics Methodology Sustainalytics, which was created in 2008, is another leader in ESG rating systems. This methodology covers the broad topics shown below: Figure 2: Sustainalytics Categories (Sustainalytics. 2014) Moyer11 Within these main topics, there are many additional factors that are covered by industry. This methodology collects data from “annual reports, financial reports, CSR reports, CSR websites, news releases, public news sources such as Bloomberg, (local) newspapers, and NGOs” (Sustainalytics. 2014). Different industries are managed by providing weighted scores for categories that are deemed to be material. Methodology for Analysis For analysis purposes, high level topics used by both MSCI and Sustainalytics were used. Due to this, there were topics left out that were deemed essential for analysis. The categories in Figure 3 were chosen to be included. Each category was chosen because it reflected a key aspect of ESG and should be included in both ESG scoring and CSR reporting. Research Questions & Predictions Based on the information gathered in the literature review section, the following research will continue to investigate different methodologies for ESG scoring and the possible necessity for future standardization of methodologies and CSR reporting. This research aims to understand what topics MSCI and Sustainalytics, two of the most used ESG rating systems, are currently assessing and whether or not it correlates with the information companies are providing, specifically on their corporate social responsibility reports. Based on previous information, the main hypotheses for this research are as follows: - H1: The topics addressed in CSR reports are correlated with the distinct metrics used by multiple top ESG scoring. - H2: The probability that a topic is addressed in CSR reports differs across topics. Figure 3: Additional Topics Moyer12 Hypothesis one looks to address the correlation between CSR reporting and ESG scoring systems. If this hypothesis is proven true it will aid in supporting the idea that reporting is being influenced by rating systems, which in turn can cause greenwashing. Hypothesis two, if proven, will show areas of scoring metrics that are neglected by the current CSR reporting that is being done by companies. With this information, a better case for standardized reporting and scoring can be made. Methodology Data Collection: In order to complete a comprehensive analysis of CSR reports across a wide range of sectors and industries, 459 CSR reports were sampled from Responsibility Reports, a large collection of up-to-date company CSR reports. The resource breaks down reports by industry and sector. In this analysis, reports from 8 sectors and 48 industries were selected to provide a broad range of companies who are producing CSR reports. By using such a broad range of company types, it allows for analysis on not only how CSR reports generally compare to scoring methods but also how this is affected by a change in industry. This insight will allow for further discussion and conclusions about whether or not reporting and scoring should be standardized by industry and sector rather than as a whole. The sample of reports collected ranged from2013 to 2022. As seen in the graph below, the large majority of reports were from 2021, meaning our assessment is of current company practices. It is crucial to assess the most current reporting practices due to the ever-changing landscape of ESG reporting. Moyer13 Figure 4: CSR Reports Sampled per Year Approach The categories for each of the documents were identified using zero-shot classification. Zero-shot classification is a type of machine learning technique where a model is able to classify instances or examples of a new class that it has not seen before without any additional training. This is achieved by the model being pre-trained on a set of classes and then leveraging the knowledge it has learned during pre-training to generalize to new classes. In other words, zero-shot classification allows a model to recognize and classify new objects or concepts, even though it has never seen examples of those objects or concepts before. The model is able to make predictions based on the patterns and representations it has learned during pre-training and its ability to generalize. Zero-shot classification is particularly useful in scenarios where obtaining labeled data for a new class is difficult or expensive, such as in the case of rare diseases or endangered species. It's also 1 1 1 1 4 7 30 84 258 42 0 50 100 150 200 250 300 0 2013 2014 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 Count of CSR Report Date Moyer14 used in multilingual natural language understanding tasks, where the model can be fine-tuned to a task in a specific language and then classify text in another language it has never seen before. It's important to note that the performance of a zero-shot model will depend on the similarity of the unseen classes to the seen classes, and the overall performance of the model can be affected by this similarity. We used BART-large-MNLI for classification. BART-large-MNLI is a pre-trained transformer-based language model which is fine-tuned on Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference (MNLI) dataset. The Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference (MNLI) dataset is a collection of natural language inference (NLI) examples in the English language. It is designed to train and evaluate models for understanding the relationship between a given premise and a given hypothesis. The premise is a statement or a fact, and the hypothesis is a statement that may or may not be true based on the premise. The task of the model is to determine if the given hypothesis is entailed, contradicted, or neutral with respect to the given premise. The MNLI dataset is unique in that it includes examples from a wide range of genres, including fiction, government, telephone, and travel. This allows models trained on the dataset to generalize to a wide range of natural language understanding tasks, as they will have been exposed to a diverse set of examples during training. The dataset is divided into three parts: the training set, the development set, and the test set. The training set is used to train models; the development set is used to tune the parameters of models, and the test set is used to evaluate the performance of models. The MNLI dataset is widely used in the NLP community as a benchmark to evaluate the performance of models on a wide range of natural language understanding tasks, and it's also one Moyer15 of the most popular datasets used to fine-tune pre-trained models, such as BERT, RoBERTa, and GPT-2. The model is trained with the denoising autoencoder objective, which is a technique for pre-training models by masking out a portion of the input and training the model to predict the original input from the masked input. Fine-tuning on MNLI allows BART-large-MNLI to perform well on a wide range of natural language inference tasks, such as recognizing text entailment, recognizing text contradiction, and recognizing neutral text. As it is a large model, it can be useful for tasks such as text generation, summarization, and text completion, with the ability to generalize to new classes without additional training. BART-large-MNLI is available on Hugging Face. Hugging Face is a company that provides an open-source platform for natural language processing (NLP). The company's main product is the Hugging Face Transformers Library, which is a collection of pre-trained state-of-the-art models for natural language understanding and generation tasks. The library includes models such as BERT, GPT-2, and RoBERTa, which are trained on a wide range of tasks including language translation, question answering, text summarization, and sentiment analysis. The models can be used to train and fine-tune natural language processing applications, such as chatbots and language translation systems. The company also provides a wide range of other NLP tools and services, such as datasets, evaluation metrics, and a model hub to share and discover models. The following steps were taken to analyze the CSR dataset using BART-large-MNLI. Moyer16 1. Pre-training: First, we downloaded the pre-trained weights of BART-large-MNLI from Hugging Face. These weights are trained on a large corpus of text data and can be used as a starting point for fine-tuning. 2. Prepare class embeddings or class attributes: Next, we prepared the class embeddings or class attributes for the unseen classes. These class embeddings or attributes were used to form a prototype of each class and classify new text based on its similarity to the prototypes. 3. Inference: Once the class embeddings were prepared, we can use the model to classify new unseen text by providing it as input and comparing the embedding of the text to the embeddings of the unseen classes. The class with the closest embedding served as the predicted class for the input text. 4. Evaluation: Finally, we evaluated the model's performance on a held-out test set to measure its accuracy and generalization ability. Advantages Zero-shot classification has several advantages over traditional supervised classification methods: 1. Scalability: Zero-shot classification can be used to classify new classes without the need for labeled training data, making it more scalable than supervised classification methods. 2. Cost-effective: Zero-shot classification reduces the need for human annotation, and makes it more cost-effective than traditional supervised methods. 3. Few-shot learning: Zero-shot classification can be used to learn new classes with very few examples, making it useful in scenarios where labeled data is scarce. Moyer17 4. Flexibility: Zero-shot classification can be applied to a wide range of natural language processing tasks, such as text classification, question answering, and machine translation. 5. Transfer learning: Zero-shot classification methods can be used to transfer knowledge learned from one task to another, allowing models to be trained more efficiently. 6. Handling unseen classes: Zero-shot classification allows to handle unseen classes during inference time, thus, it can be applied to real-world applications where new classes are constantly being added. 7. Better Generalization: Zero-shot classification, if trained properly, can generalize better to unseen classes. Limitations Zero-shot classification, although a powerful approach, has several limitations: 1. Limited generalization: Zero-shot classification relies on class embeddings or class attributes, which may not always be available or may not provide enough information to accurately classify unseen text. This can lead to poor generalization and low accuracy in unseen classes. 2. Assumptions on unseen classes: Zero-shot classification assumes that unseen classes are similar to seen classes in terms of their embeddings or attributes. However, this assumption may not always hold true in practice, leading to poor performance in unseen classes. Moyer18 3. Limited interpretability: Zero-shot classification models are typically complex and may not provide clear explanations for their predictions, making it difficult to understand why the model is making certain classifications. 4. Lack of fine-tuning: Zero-shot classification without fine-tuning may not adapt to the specific task at hand and improve performance. 5. Data-Hungry: Zero-shot classification requires a large amount of labeled data to train the model and learn the embeddings or attributes of the classes. In scenarios where labeled data is scarce, zero-shot classification may not be practical. Despite these limitations, zero-shot classification remains a promising approach for natural language processing tasks, and researchers continue to develop new methods to improve its performance and overcome its limitations. Analysis After conducting the approach and comparing CSR reports versus the topics covered in MSCI, Sustainalytics, and the created topic list, the full dataset was able to be analyzed using SPSS Statistics. After exploring the initial dataset, it was clear that there would be some manipulation of the data necessary to find meaningful results.

--- SOURCE 3: The case for adopting established ESG standards over creating your ... ---
URL: https://trellis.net/article/case-adopting-established-esg-standards-over-creating-your-own-framework/

SUMMARY:
Standardization remains a critical component of ESG reporting, ensuring that stakeholders can make meaningful comparisons and informed decisions

FULL CONTENT:
![Trellis](https://trellis.net/wp-content/themes/greenbiz/static/logo-trellis.svg)
![Trellis](https://trellis.net/wp-content/themes/greenbiz/static/logo-trellis-formerly-gb.svg)

![](https://trellis.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/greenfin_TI25_website_feature_image_1200x630.png?w=300)
![](https://trellis.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/greenbiz26_website_feature_image_1200x630-1.png?w=300)
![](https://trellis.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Circularity_Trellis_Impact_website_feature_image.png?w=300)

# The case for adopting established ESG standards over creating your own framework

Sponsored: Choosing custom ESG reporting over common standards invites scrutiny, harming firms. Learn how to embrace standards for credible disclosure benefits. [Read More](https://trellis.net/article/case-adopting-established-esg-standards-over-creating-your-own-framework/)

![Image courtesy of Shutterstock.](https://trellis.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/shutterstock_2161864639.jpg?w=1024)

*Image courtesy of Shutterstock.*

*This article is sponsored by [IsoMetrix.](https://www.isometrix.com/?utm_source=google&utm_medium=paid_search&utm_campaign=isometrix&utm_id=1&utm_term=isometrix&utm_content=google_ads&gclid=CjwKCAiA3aeqBhBzEiwAxFiOBo86mMT2DXuKpc6nFO2Pz-VX4xUIn1HvbT68Yc-f5FJtacC2Qw_ZgBoC17MQAvD_BwE)*

Stakeholder demands for businesses to disclose non-financial metrics has increased exponentially over the past few years. Primarily, this has centered on data around environmental and social impact, as well as good corporate governance — or how committed they are to environmental, social and governance (ESG). While ESG has gained more mindshare in business and investor circles since 2020, its origins go back much further to concepts such as corporate social responsibility and environmental sustainability.

To help companies report social and environmental information, multiple frameworks emerged, including the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI), Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB), Taskforce on Carbon Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), CDP, and others. Some of these focus on climate impact, while others capture metrics that span the entire ESG spectrum.

While by now most organizations looking to disclose ESG information through reporting are familiar with these reporting methodologies, I was surprised to learn that some major companies are instead using frameworks they created themselves. Typically, the justification would be that the mainstream standards were not fully applicable to their businesses, and that because some items were immaterial, the whole standard did not work for their company.

Given that every company is different with a unique ESG context, using a homegrown reporting framework might sound tempting. But organizations looking to create a world-class ESG strategy and reporting program must avoid this temptation and embrace the formal frameworks.

### **Standardization enables relevance**

Just like with the Generally Approved Accounting Principles (GAAP) in the U.S. and the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) in over 100 other countries and territories, carbon accounting and ESG reporting need to be standardized to have relevance. While dollars and cents are binary, concepts such as depreciation, amortization and revenue recognition are measured in a number of different ways, which is detrimental to shareholders and other stakeholders if not done in good faith. Similarly, when ESG metrics are chosen and omitted at the will of the reporting organization, there is a risk of disclosing only part of the story – usually the side that favors the reporting organization. Regardless of intent, observers will question if they are getting the full story of a company’s performance if data is incomplete relative to their expectations.

Assuming all reporting is done in good faith, making sense of the nuances in ESG reporting can still be a mixed bag. A 2021 study found that [only 24 percent of retail investor respondents could identify the meaning of the ESG acronym](https://www.finrafoundation.org/sites/finrafoundation/files/Consumer-Insights-Money-and-Investing.pdf). While its familiarity may have increased since then, inconsistent reporting doesn’t help investors, or other stakeholders for that matter, make informed decisions about a company’s stature. As investors will likely find little value in the uncertainty, non-standardized ESG reporting unfortunately becomes little more than a PR activity with limited benefit.

### Top ESG performers embrace standards and frameworks

Companies that excel in their ESG strategies often demonstrate stronger business performance as well. Studies indicate that ESG high-performers have [less volatile earnings over time](https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/10-reasons-to-care-about-esg-investing-bank-of-america-2019-9-1028557439), [benefit from lower costs of capital](https://www.ey.com/en_id/real-world-strategy/how-companies-can-link-esg-to-long-term-value) and [see more productivity from their employees](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/McKinsey/Business%20Functions/Strategy%20and%20Corporate%20Finance/Our%20Insights/Five%20ways%20that%20ESG%20creates%20value/Five-ways-that-ESG-creates-value.ashx). The businesses that reap the most benefits from their ESG reputations are the ones that make it easy for investors to compare them against other potential investments. Part of this is measured through [ESG ratings](https://trellis.net/article/esg-score-calculation-how-find-your-companys-rating-part-1), which have different methodologies but mainly center around company-released disclosures.

The top-performing companies also usually align to multiple of the common standards and frameworks, to provide well-rounded views of how they approach ESG while disclosing both strengths and weaknesses. When getting started, a reliable approach is to take one standard and use it as a basis for assessing topics material to your organization. For example, there is an area in GRI reporting that asks about water treatment — if your business does not treat water, it can be deemed immaterial in the company’s report.

With there being many standards and frameworks against which an organization can measure itself, it can be tough to decide which ones to begin with. identifying which ones to begin with. A good place to start is to identify the frameworks your company is required to report on. New ESG and climate disclosure regulations in Europe, Canada, Australia and California mean that if you or your supply chain partners do business in any of these places, then you will have to report. If your organization does not have mandatory disclosure requirements, then it will be a good exercise to review which standards are most relevant or common in your industry. Checking methodologies your industry peers or competitors use is often a good way to see what’s common in your industry but also some internet research can provide relevant guidance. An ESG consultancy firm may also be of assistance here, as they will have a broad base of exposure to different companies and industries and are often well-positioned to be able to offer advice in this regard.

### ESG and sustainability disclosure is here to stay

While some companies may hope that ESG and sustainability disclosures will quietly go away, market momentum suggests that reporting expectations and requirements will only become more formalized and enduring. Recently, a counsel at a transportation and manufacturing company told me that the motivation for his company to improve its ESG reporting came from inquiries it received from insurance firms and banks. There were no penalties for his firm’s lack of robust ESG metrics, but there is a good chance the insurance and loan rates he received were higher than if he could have shown evidence of a comprehensive and actively implemented ESG strategy.

A similar scenario exists within private investing. With venture capital dollars being much harder to earn than in years past, investors are taking ESG-related risks into consideration with how to allocate their capital. A leader from PepsiCo said during a conference recently that sustainability is a variable used when gauging bid prices in mergers and acquisitions, for example.

Cost and access to capital can be effective motivators for businesses to get started with ESG reporting. Fortunately, voluntary reporting holds less liability than mandates and allows organizations to explain gaps in reporting. With the focus on continuous improvement related to disclosures and reporting infrastructure, companies can learn from each other’s best practices without the fear of punitive measures. Ideally, voluntary reporting will help drive companies to meaningful change and improvement of their overall ESG performance.

While the idea of companies developing their own ESG reporting frameworks may seem appealing in light of their unique circumstances, it ultimately falls short of delivering the transparency and consistency needed in the rapidly evolving world of ESG disclosures. Standardization remains a critical component of ESG reporting, ensuring that stakeholders can make meaningful comparisons and informed decisions. As global regulatory requirements for ESG reporting grow, embracing recognized standards becomes not only a necessity but a competitive advantage.

--- SOURCE 4: 4 reasons why standardizing ESG information is key for true impact ---
URL: https://blog.worldfavor.com/4-reasons-why-standardizing-esg-information-is-key-for-true-impact

SUMMARY:
Standardization makes data comparable  This often results in the ESG scoring of the same company can vary widely, making it hard to interpret what the information actually implies. On the other hand, solely relying on companies' sustainability reports to screen their ESG maturity is not the way to go either.

FULL CONTENT:
![Logo+Wordmark_Black](https://blog.worldfavor.com/hubfs/Logo%2BWordmark_Black.svg)

# 4 reasons why standardizing ESG information is key for true impact

![4 reasons why standardizing ESG information is key for true impact](https://blog.worldfavor.com/hubfs/Blog%20pictures/Why%20Standardizing%20ESG%20disclosure%20is%20key%20for%20true%20impact.jpg)

Today, many investors are unable to properly pinpoint a company’s environmental, social, and governance (ESG) performance because of the lack of standardized ESG information. ESG experts are underlining that [ESG performance is still difficult when trying to compare between companies](https://www.spglobal.com/marketintelligence/en/news-insights/latest-news-headlines/lack-of-standardized-esg-data-may-hide-material-risks-oecd-says-60541261) as there is a wealth of different methodologic, metrics and weightings out there –  making it difficult for investors to make sound and informed decisions. However, the absence of standardized ESG data is the biggest threat to properly evaluating a company’s performance on ESG criteria. 

Luckily, this doesn’t have to be the case. In this blog, we will talk about how to standardize your ESG screening process to unlock the true potential of ESG investing!

### **Why is Standardized ESG information important?**

Although more investors and asset owners are beginning to unlock the potential of ESG investing, the lack of data standardization is still very real. Research has found that [even large, established investing institutions are integrating ESG screening into their investment processes that are not relying on systematic, consistent, and tested methodology](https://www.oecd.org/finance/ESG-Investing-Practices-Progress-Challenges.pdf) – making it difficult to effectively evaluate and compare companies’ ESG practices and risks. A recent report from OECD showed significant differences in the ESG scoring for the same company between different rating providers such as Bloomberg, MSCI, and Refinitiv. 

To truly be able to understand a company’s sustainability practices – accessing standardized and comparable disclosures is a must. Below, we’ve listed some of the key reasons why standardized ESG information is important for investors seeking to improve their ESG investments and truly contribute capital toward sustainable finance: 

#### **1. Spot differences, risks and opportunities**

As mentioned, the absence of standardized ESG data is the biggest threat for asset managers to properly evaluate a company’s performance on ESG criteria. Investment decisions based on information that is neither comprehensive nor comparable between companies won’t provide the true picture – rather the contrary. Without a standardized and systemized process based on sound science, risks can easily fall through the cracks and go unnoticed long enough for them to become a problem.

#### **2. Standardization makes data comparable**

Different ESG rating providers, such as [MSCI ESG Ratings](https://www.msci.com/our-solutions/esg-investing/esg-ratings) and [Sustainalytics](https://www.sustainalytics.com/esg-ratings), usually have their own sourcing process and scoring methodologies. This often results in the ESG scoring of the same company can vary widely, making it hard to interpret what the information actually implies.

On the other hand, solely relying on companies’ sustainability reports to screen their ESG maturity is not the way to go either. Most sustainability reports often lack transparency and are rather filled with holes and well-chosen case studies promoting their sustainability achievements.

Don’t risk missing out on important insights. Set up your own, standardized ESG management process to get ESG information you can rely on. [Let us explain how you can benefit from using Worldfavor as your ESG management tool!](https://content.worldfavor.com/demo-request-sustainability-platform)

#### **3. Adds clarity to what is expected of companies**

[As Worldfavor’s CEO Andreas Liljendahl pointed out](https://blog.worldfavor.com/sustainability-chat-with-andreas-liljendahl-worldfavor) “In sustainability there is a jungle of standards, frameworks and initiatives that companies are expected to follow and know how to navigate in. It makes the whole area very complex for companies to, firstly, decide what they should report on, and secondly, to communicate that information to their stakeholders”.

Don’t leave your ESG information providers by themselves in the reporting jungle. Make it easy for them to know when, how and what to report. By standardizing the process, you’re not only crystalizing what is expected by them – but also helping them to take control of their own sustainability journey.

#### **4. Control of measuring, evaluating and describing the portfolio**

Lastly, see a systematic ESG process as a great tool to access reliable ​​data-driven insights that you can measure, evaluate and describe your portfolio companies. While it might be tempting to rely on secondary data sources, such as ESG rating providers or sustainability reports, having your own ESG management system is the only way to be in full control of what is measured and how.

### **Standardize your ESG process in Worldfavor Sustainable Finance or Worldfavor Sustainable Investments:**

Worldfavor’s dedicated sustainability platform is designed to help asset owners with their general ESG reporting, but also with their sector-specific requirements and challenges. That’s why we have two different solutions to fit your needs: [**Sustainable Investments**](https://worldfavor.com/sustainable-investments/) and [**Sustainable Finance**](https://worldfavor.com/sustainable-finance/)***.*** Easily access ESG information from your portfolio companies or customers. [Talk to one of our account executives today to learn more about our different solutions and how they can help you reach your ESG goals!](https://content.worldfavor.com/demo-request-sustainability-platform)

URL: https://www.thecorporategovernanceinstitute.com/insights/guides/whats-the-difference-between-esg-reporting-standards-and-frameworks/?srsltid=AfmBOop_-c28pTgPzZHHEP3XXRYDcipmAmEp9G56D6L_eq9slbzvbv5G

SUMMARY:
ESG reporting standards are more technical. They give specific requirements, like precise metrics for reporting each topic.

DESCRIPTION: A diagram lists four key components of common ESG reporting frameworks, each represented by numbered hexagons and corresponding colored bars: the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI), the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB), the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), and the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: The demand for standardized ESG metrics is crucial, as it enables investors to reliably compare companies and make informed decisions based on consistent data.
Efforts to harmonize ESG reporting requirements across regions and industries are ongoing, aimed at reducing discrepancies and improving comparability of sustainability disclosures.
Regulatory bodies like the ISSB and the EU are pushing for standardized ESG frameworks, enhancing accountability and transparency in corporate sustainability reporting.
